title: Pega Robotic Process Automation (RPA) — Complete Guide
content:
# Pega Robotic Process Automation (RPA) — Complete Guide

## What Is Pega RPA?

Pega Robotic Process Automation (RPA) is Pega's technology for automating repetitive, rule-based tasks that involve interacting with desktop applications, web browsers, legacy systems, and other software interfaces. Pega RPA uses software robots (bots) to mimic human actions — clicking buttons, entering data, reading screens, copying information between systems — without modifying the underlying applications.

Pega RPA is part of the broader Pega Infinity platform and integrates natively with Pega's case management, decisioning (NBA), and workflow capabilities. This makes it unique compared to standalone RPA tools like UiPath or Automation Anywhere.

## Key Components

### 1. Pega Robot Studio
Robot Studio is the design environment for building automations (robots). It provides a visual drag-and-drop interface for creating automation flows.

**Key features:**
- **Screen Interrogation**: Automatically identifies UI elements (buttons, fields, tables) in target applications
- **Recording**: Records user actions and converts them to automation steps
- **Toolbox**: Pre-built automation components for common actions (click, type, read, wait, Excel operations, PDF processing)
- **Debugging**: Step-through debugging with variable inspection
- **Version Control**: Built-in version management for automation packages

**Supported target technologies:**
- Windows desktop applications (Win32, .NET, WPF, Java Swing/SWT)
- Web browsers (Chrome, Edge, Firefox, IE)
- Citrix / Remote Desktop (via image recognition and OCR)
- Terminal emulators (mainframe green screens)
- SAP GUI
- Excel, PDF, email clients

### 2. Pega Robot Manager
Robot Manager is the server-side management console for deploying, monitoring, and managing robots across the organization.

**Capabilities:**
- **Deployment**: Push automation packages to attended or unattended robots
- **Scheduling**: Schedule unattended robots to run at specific times or triggers
- **Monitoring**: Real-time dashboard showing robot status, execution logs, errors
- **Credential Management**: Securely store and manage credentials used by robots
- **Queue Management**: Manage work queues for unattended robots
- **Analytics**: Robot performance metrics, ROI tracking, utilization reports

### 3. Pega Robot Runtime
The Robot Runtime is the lightweight agent installed on user workstations (attended) or virtual machines (unattended) that executes the automations.

## Attended vs Unattended Robots

### Attended Robots (Desktop Automation)
- Run on the user's desktop alongside the human worker
- Triggered manually by the user or automatically when certain conditions are met
- Assist the user by automating repetitive parts of their workflow
- User maintains control and can intervene at any point
- Common use cases: data entry assistance, screen scraping, form filling, copy-paste between systems
- **Deployment**: Installed on individual user workstations

### Unattended Robots (Server Automation)
- Run on dedicated virtual machines without human intervention
- Triggered by schedules, API calls, or queue-based work items
- Execute end-to-end processes autonomously
- Typically run 24/7 for high-volume repetitive tasks
- Common use cases: batch data processing, report generation, system-to-system data transfer, overnight reconciliation
- **Deployment**: Run on VMs managed by Robot Manager

### Hybrid Model
Pega supports hybrid attended + unattended scenarios where:
- An attended robot collects initial information from the user
- Hands off complex processing to an unattended robot
- Returns results to the user's attended robot when done

## Integration with Pega Platform

### Case Management Integration
Pega RPA integrates directly with Pega case management:
- Robots can be triggered from case flows and assignments
- Robot results (data, status) feed back into the case
- Case steps can wait for robot completion before proceeding
- **How to configure**: In App Studio, add a "Robot automation" step to your case flow. Select the automation package and map input/output parameters.

### Next-Best-Action (NBA) Integration
RPA can be part of Pega's AI-driven decisioning:
- NBA can recommend robotic automation for specific tasks
- Robots execute the recommended actions
- Results feed back into the decisioning model

### Workforce Intelligence Integration
Pega Workforce Intelligence (WFI) analyzes how employees work to identify automation opportunities:
- WFI monitors desktop activity (anonymized) to find repetitive patterns
- Suggests processes that are good candidates for RPA
- Quantifies potential time savings
- Directly feeds into Robot Studio for automation development

## Building a Robot — Step by Step

### Step 1: Create a New Project in Robot Studio
1. Open Robot Studio
2. File → New Project
3. Name the project and select target application type
4. Choose the runtime environment (attended/unattended)

### Step 2: Interrogate the Target Application
1. Open the target application
2. In Robot Studio, use the Interrogation tool (magnifying glass icon)
3. Hover over UI elements to identify them
4. Robot Studio detects element properties (name, type, coordinates, accessibility attributes)
5. Verified elements are added to the Object Repository

### Step 3: Build the Automation Flow
1. Drag automation components from the Toolbox onto the canvas
2. Common components:
   - **Click**: Click a button or link
   - **Type Into**: Enter text into a field
   - **Read**: Read text from a screen element
   - **Wait**: Wait for an element to appear/disappear
   - **If/Else**: Conditional branching
   - **Loop**: Iterate over items (table rows, list items)
   - **Excel**: Read/write Excel files
   - **Email**: Send/read emails
3. Connect components to create the flow
4. Map variables between steps

### Step 4: Add Error Handling
1. Wrap risky steps in Try-Catch blocks
2. Add retry logic for transient failures (network delays, slow UI)
3. Configure timeout values for wait steps
4. Add screenshot capture on error for debugging
5. Define fallback behavior (skip, retry, alert human, abort)

### Step 5: Test and Debug
1. Run the automation in debug mode
2. Step through each action
3. Inspect variables at each step
4. Verify the automation handles edge cases (missing data, unexpected popups, application errors)

### Step 6: Deploy
1. Package the automation in Robot Studio
2. Upload to Robot Manager
3. Assign to attended users or unattended VMs
4. Configure schedules (for unattended)
5. Monitor execution in Robot Manager dashboard

## Troubleshooting Common RPA Issues

### 1. Element Not Found
**Symptom**: Robot fails with "Element not found" or "Cannot locate control"
**Common causes**:
- Target application UI changed (button moved, renamed, restructured)
- Screen resolution differs between development and production machines
- Application loading delay — element not yet rendered
- Pop-up or dialog blocking the target element
**Fix**:
- Re-interrogate the element in Robot Studio
- Use more robust selectors (accessibility attributes > coordinates)
- Add Wait steps before interacting with elements
- Handle pop-ups with Try-Catch + dismiss logic

### 2. Timing / Synchronization Issues
**Symptom**: Robot clicks too fast, enters data into wrong fields, or misses elements
**Common causes**:
- Application is slower in production than in development
- Network latency for web applications
- Background processes slowing the target application
**Fix**:
- Add explicit Wait steps (wait for element visible, wait for element enabled)
- Avoid fixed sleep/delay — use dynamic waits
- Set appropriate timeout values (5-30 seconds depending on application)

### 3. Citrix / Remote Desktop Issues
**Symptom**: Robot cannot identify elements in Citrix or RDP sessions
**Common causes**:
- Citrix/RDP renders the application as an image stream, not native UI elements
- OCR accuracy issues with low-resolution rendering
**Fix**:
- Use Pega's Citrix automation adapter
- Use image recognition + OCR for element identification
- Increase Citrix display resolution
- Use coordinate-based clicking as last resort (fragile)

### 4. Credential Failures
**Symptom**: Robot fails to log into target application
**Common causes**:
- Password expired or changed
- Credential vault not accessible
- Multi-factor authentication (MFA) blocking automated login
**Fix**:
- Update credentials in Robot Manager's credential vault
- Configure service accounts with long-lived passwords
- Request MFA exemption for robot service accounts
- Use API-based authentication where possible instead of UI login

### 5. Robot Disconnected / Offline
**Symptom**: Robot Manager shows robot as disconnected
**Common causes**:
- Robot Runtime service stopped on the machine
- Network connectivity issue between robot and Robot Manager
- Machine rebooted and Runtime didn't auto-start
**Fix**:
- Check the Robot Runtime service on the machine
- Verify network connectivity to Robot Manager server
- Configure Robot Runtime as a Windows service with auto-start
- Set up monitoring alerts for robot disconnection

### 6. PEGA0096 — Robot Runtime Error
This is the Pega platform alert for RPA runtime failures. See the alert in PegaALERT.log for details. Common causes include UI changes in target applications, element timeouts, and application crashes during robot execution.

## Best Practices

### Design
- **Keep robots small and focused**: One robot per process, not one mega-robot
- **Use the Page Object pattern**: Separate UI element definitions from automation logic
- **Build in error recovery**: Every robot should handle common failure scenarios
- **Parameterize**: Use variables for environment-specific values (URLs, file paths, credentials)
- **Log extensively**: Add logging at key steps for debugging production issues

### Testing
- **Test on production-like environments**: Screen resolution, OS version, application version must match production
- **Test with realistic data volumes**: Performance can degrade with large datasets
- **Test edge cases**: Empty fields, special characters, maximum-length data, network failures
- **Regression test after application updates**: UI changes are the #1 cause of robot failures

### Deployment
- **Use separate credentials for robots**: Don't share human user credentials
- **Monitor robot health proactively**: Set up alerts for failures, not just successes
- **Plan for maintenance windows**: Target application updates will often break robots
- **Scale unattended robots horizontally**: Add more VMs rather than running more robots per VM

### Security
- **Use Robot Manager credential vault**: Never hardcode credentials in automations
- **Apply least-privilege principle**: Robot accounts should only have the permissions they need
- **Audit robot actions**: Keep detailed logs of what robots do for compliance
- **Encrypt sensitive data**: Use Pega's encryption for any sensitive data handled by robots

## Pega RPA vs Other RPA Tools

| Feature | Pega RPA | UiPath | Automation Anywhere |
|---|---|---|---|
| Native Pega Integration | Yes (built-in) | Via connector | Via connector |
| Case Management | Native | Requires integration | Requires integration |
| AI/Decisioning (NBA) | Native | Separate | Separate |
| Workforce Intelligence | Built-in | Separate tool | Separate tool |
| Low-code Design | Robot Studio | Studio/StudioX | Bot Creator |
| Attended + Unattended | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Citrix Support | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Licensing | Part of Pega license | Separate license | Separate license |

Pega RPA's key advantage is native integration — robots are part of the same platform as case management, decisioning, and workflow. No separate middleware or API integration needed.

## Licensing and Editions

Pega RPA is available as:
- **Pega Robotic Automation**: Full attended + unattended RPA with Robot Studio and Robot Manager
- **Part of Pega Infinity**: Included in Pega Infinity bundles with case management and decisioning
- **Standalone**: Can be purchased separately from the full Pega platform

Licensing is typically based on the number of robot instances (attended workstations + unattended VMs).
